title: Ensure Trusted Launch is enabled on Virtual Machines

input_row.title: Ensure Trusted Launch is enabled on Virtual Machines
input_row.description: When Secure Boot and vTPM are enabled together, they provide a strong foundation for protecting your VM from boot attacks. For example, if an attacker attempts to replace the bootloader with a malicious version, Secure Boot will prevent the VM from booting. If the attacker is able to bypass Secure Boot and install a malicious bootloader, vTPM can be used to detect the intrusion and alert you.
input_row.rationale: Secure Boot and vTPM work together to protect your VM from a variety of boot attacks, including bootkits, rootkits, and firmware rootkits. Not enabling Trusted Launch in Azure VM can lead to increased vulnerability to rootkits and boot-level malware, reduced ability to detect and prevent unauthorized changes to the boot process, and a potential compromise of system integrity and data security.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to Virtual Machines 2. For each VM, under Settings, click on Configuration on the left blade 3. Under Security Type, make sure security type is not standard and if it is Trusted Launch Virtual Machines then make sure Enable Secure Boot & Enable vTPM are checked Audit from Azure CLI Run the following command to list VM names and security profile settings: az vm list --query [*].[name,securityProfile] For each VM, ensure that securityType is set to TrustedLaunch, uefiSettings.secureBootEnabled is set to true, and uefiSettings.vTpmEnabled is set to true. Audit from PowerShell Run the following command to list VMs: Get-AzVm Run the following command to get the VM in a resource group with a given name: $vm = Get-AzVm -ResourceGroupName -Name Run the following command to get the security profile settings for the VM: $vm.SecurityProfile Ensure that SecurityType is set to TrustedLaunch. Run the following command to get the UEFI settings for the VM: $vm.SecurityProfile.UefiSettings Ensure that SecureBootEnabled and VTpmEnabled are set to True. Repeat for each VM. Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: c95b54ad-0614-4633-ab29-104b01235cbf - Name: 'Virtual Machine should have TrustedLaunch enabled'
input_row.remediation: Note: Trusted launch on existing virtual machines (VMs) is currently not supported for Azure Generation 1 VMs Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to Virtual Machines. 2. For each VM, under Settings, click on Configuration on the left blade. 3. Under Security Type, select 'Trusted Launch Virtual Machines'. 4. Make sure Enable Secure Boot & Enable vTPM are checked. 5. Click on Apply. Remediate from Azure CLI Ensure that it is safe to change the state of the VM. Run the following command to deallocate the VM: az vm deallocate --resource-group --name Run the following command to update the VM, setting security type to TrustedLaunch and enabling secure boot and vTPM: az vm update --resource-group --name --enable-secure- boot true --enable-vtpm true --security-type TrustedLaunch Run the following command to restart the VM: az vm start --resource-group --name Repeat for each VM requiring remediation. Remediate from PowerShell Ensure that it is safe to change the state of the VM. Run the following command to stop the VM: Stop-AzVm -ResourceGroupName -Name -Force Run the following command to get the VM in a resource group with a given name: $vm = Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName -Name Run the following command to update the VM, setting security type to TrustedLaunch and enabling secure boot and vTPM: Update-AzVm -ResourceGroupName -VM $vm -EnableSecureBoot 1 - EnableVtpm 1 -SecurityType TrustedLaunch Run the following command to start the VM: Start-AzVm -ResourceGroupName -Name Repeat for each VM requiring remediation.
input_row.impact: Secure Boot and vTPM are not currently supported for Azure Generation 1 VMs. IMPORTANT: Before enabling Secure Boot and vTPM on a Generation 2 VM which does not already have both enabled, it is highly recommended to create a restore point of the VM prior to remediation.
input_row.default_value: On Azure Generation 2 VMs, vTPM is enabled by default. Secure Boot is not enabled by default.
input_row.justification: The control pertains to Azure-specific features (Trusted Launch, Secure Boot, vTPM) which do not have direct equivalents in AWS. AWS does not support a feature called Trusted Launch, and thus, the audit cannot be automated using AWS APIs or CLI as described. The first AI opinion incorrectly suggested an automated approach using AWS APIs that do not apply to this control.
input_row.manual_summary: 1. **Review AWS EC2 Security Features:** - Navigate to the AWS Management Console. - Go to the EC2 Dashboard and select the instances you wish to review. 2. **Verify Security Configurations:** - Check the AMI used for each instance to ensure it is a hardened image (e.g., Amazon Linux 2, Windows Server with security updates). - Ensure that the instance metadata service is configured to use IMDSv2 for enhanced security. 3. **Inspect Instance Security Settings:** - Review the security groups and IAM roles associated with each instance to ensure they follow best practices for least privilege and network security. - Confirm that the instance does not have unnecessary open ports and that it uses secure protocols for communication. 4. **Confirm Compliance:** - Document the security settings and configurations for each instance. - Ensure that all instances meet the organization's security policies and standards.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control is suitable for an automated audit due to the following reasons:
- The audit details include specific Azure CLI and PowerShell commands that can retrieve the necessary VM security properties, specifically for validating Trusted Launch settings.
- The Azure API can be queried to assess the security profiles for VMs, which includes features for Secure Boot and vTPM.
- Both Azure CLI and Azure SDK for Python provide methods and commands to fetch VM configurations that align with the requirements.

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_vm_trusted_launch_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
The automated program would perform the following checks:

1. **Azure API Calls or Azure SDK for Python methods to use:**
   - Use the `compute` namespace in Azure SDK for Python, specifically the `VirtualMachinesOperations` class to retrieve VM properties.
   - `az vm list` command can be run through Azure CLI to fetch VM security profiles.

2. **Azure CLI commands (if applicable):**
   - Use the command: `az vm list --query [*].[name,securityProfile]` to get a list of VMs and their security settings.

3. **Specific resources to enumerate:**
   - The program will enumerate all Virtual Machines in the specified subscription or resource groups.

4. **Exact configuration properties/settings to validate:**
   - For each VM:
     - `securityType` should equal `TrustedLaunch`
     - `uefiSettings.secureBootEnabled` should be `true`
     - `uefiSettings.vTpmEnabled` should be `true`

5. **Clear Pass/Fail criteria with expected values:**
   - **Pass Criteria:** All environments show that `securityType` is set to `TrustedLaunch`, `secureBootEnabled` is `true`, and `vTpmEnabled` is `true`.
   - **Fail Criteria:** Any VM that does not meet these configurations will be flagged.

This comprehensive approach enables systematic auditing of the Trusted Launch settings for Azure Virtual Machines to ensure compliance with the CIS benchmark.
